item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't like it. What do we have? The Foundation founds some items (a photo, a book and a phone) mentioning Danhorn, tries to find Danhorn and fails. Then finds other non-existent/disappeared towns and… that's all. There is nothing more. We have so small information that we can't even hypothesize what happened to Danhorn and others, we don't even know where they should be! These towns have disappeared? Due to incident, sabotage or deliberate attack? Why do archive contain nothing? Everything about these towns is erased? But why do we have a picture of Danhorn library? Or these items are from a parallel universe where Danhorn is a very real town? Or it's a hoax? But who would fake these evidence and why?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the vagueness of this article. It gives you just enough information to conclude that something is definitely happening and develop some hypotheses, but not enough to make a clear decision about what it is. The text messages are a great example- "confirmed status of Danhorn" and nothing else, including the sender or recipient. Was this person checking their handiwork, or just investigating the phenomenon? How much did they or their contact know about the status of Danhorn? Where the hell are they, and why did they leave their cell phone behind? We don't know, but I'm sure most readers have a few ideas already.

This one doesn't make you feel sympathetic, or scared, or disturbed, or even angry. It just makes you think, and it does that with great efficacy. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'The prohibition on destructive testing applies to the cover and front pages of the book, but does not apply to the story itself.'

That was beautiful xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I clicked part of the cell phone description, and my map app opened and showed me Lat. 0, Lon. 0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's fine, and i like the concept of a town that might or might not exist, but it doesn't go anywhere interesting enough for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staxx, I'm not sure I understand your objection. I imagined that Foundation investigators had performed internet, white pages and census searches for Ms. Louisa Watson. They got some hits on the name, but when they investigated those hits, they found that no one with that name was living in Iowa in that time-frame. You made me think about it long enough that I'm going to make a quick edit to make it flow a little better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this article. Its mystery is subtly woven and delicately spun across a sparse frame. Such a light-handed touch is very impressive, especially considering that it's the author's debut. +1

One quibble I have with the article is this statement:

Three Louisa Watsons were located, but none had lived in Iowa.

What the hell does that mean? I could probably spend a day knocking on random doors in my local area and locate three Louisa Watsons who had never lived in Iowa. It is a rather common name. I don't understand what this statement adds at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wishun, that last part about "towns on their way in from somewhere else" is a great angle I hadn't considered. Reminds me of the Langoliers effect. I was originally thinking possibilities should include government conspiracy, hoax and genuine physical anomaly, where the that anomaly would have to alter space-time in a way that erases the towns and most, but not all physical evidence and memories. If the erasure was complete, then no one would know, so that's not much of a SCP. If there was tons of physical evidence, including GPS coordinates, and everyone had a clear memory it narrows down the possible explanations, but is somehow less interesting in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is almost like a framework for an scp, and it's left up to the reader to fill out what's really going on with their best geuss. But I mean that in a good way. Much like the evidence in-universe, this article is open to speculation and multiple interpretations.

For myself, I can't decide if these are towns that have been nearly erased, incompletly pushed out of reality; or are they on their way in from somewhere else, with some bits and echoes arriving before the rest is fully real?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank community members that provided comments on my first SCP. The positive comments are encouraging and the constructive criticism really did guide me to improve the article. For those that like the entire concept, I can't do much about that. I'm going to let this one rest and work on another SCP now.

There were three reasons I gravitated to this idea:

  1. I have a terrible memory and when I return to my home state, I find myself unable to navigate to places I know exist. In fact, last time I returned I couldn't find one of the apartments I lived in.
  2. I'm a fan of H. P. Lovecraft and the inability to find places where one has been in the past is a recurring theme in some of his stories.
  3. There are thousands of articles out there and in order to add something to the site, I felt I needed to go in a slightly different direction. The twist is that the objects are completely mundane, but when combined they suggest something that is dangerous. The Foundation hasn't quite figured it out, but understands that society can not handle the truth, whatever it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, especially the part about residents giving conflicting directions. There's something quintessentially Midwestern American about that factoid that really drives this one home.

+ show block
Headcanony rambling

I'd like to think that the presence of the book, given its title, somehow causes places and people to vanish. If reading the novel actually caused some sort of obsession with nothingness, or something like that, which then became manifest after enough people caught on to it. Not sure that would add anything to this, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Simple, charming and to the point.
I love this one quite a lot. It has perfect tone and atmosphere. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just barely qualifies as anomalous in my mind. The two addenda at the end are the only reason I didn't novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Sketch - I agree. Thanks for the input. I've updated in a manner which avoids drawing in other SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't have to do that. It could be the same length, just swapping the sentence about setting the other towns up as SCPs to "[town name] is now under consideration as an extensive of SCP-1971. or something like that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little of this may be laziness on my part, but mostly I don't like long SCPs. I like the short story art form - trying to get the biggest story into the shortest amount of words. If I have to start describing all of the new evidence I will end up dissatisfied. By what other means can I let the reader know that things are escalating without quadrupling the length?

Disclaimer: I had no intention of writing these other SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Better. Downvote cancelled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would remove the bit about adding the new town as a seperate SCP, as it implies a seperate-ness. I think it would be stronger to try and present the idea of a single, large phenomenon of towns that might or might not exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added two addenda to transform this subtle mystery SCP into something more threatening. Did it work or did I shoot myself in the foot?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually really like the new picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr Cuddles critique has caused me to take another run at photo shop. My skills are improving and I'm much happier with this photo. I changed the city hall into a library. And yes, that building actually is a library. It is the library in my home town.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like reading tales. It's just that when so many pop up at once, it gets a bit destructive and confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, you got me there Dr Cuddles. I started to think that something was wrong earlier today but couldn't put my finger on it. I started with a different picture, but it turned out to be too hard to photo shop. Now that you mentioned this, I'm back to the grindstone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, it's OK like that. The objects contained are the three items, so that's what the containment procedure should be about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the atmosphere of this one and it was enjoyable to read. I would not give it +10, but I think it deserves +1. Funny Eskobar mentionned King, because it reminds me a bit of the Dark Tower with it's alternate universes/books.

Another mystery about Danhorn: if it's a small village, how come it has a CITY hall…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the uncertainty of what is going on in this town. My canon is that this is a It's a Good Life-type issue, and this is all that was left behind.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You got a problem with tales punk?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how sparse and not-trying-to-be-scary it is. It's a very "Well, this is the best we could manage with what little data we have. *shrug*" article that leaves the reader to draw their own conclusions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with Drewbear on all counts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a nice little puzzlement of an SCP. I'd rather this than another "it's scary so it kills you" SCP.
Or more zombies.
Or viruses.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. I agree with Scantron on the style. The article doesn't quite spark my intrigue, though. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's cause of the New Year's contest, son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seemed to be a calmer kind of SCP, and an unusual one too! I did enjoy it, and it made a change to the usual mega creepy stuff. +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it could go either way, actually. I mean, the Foundation actually HAS the items, whereas the town can't be located.

Also, I rather like that this is built on mysteriousness rather than scare. It makes it suitably different from Samothrace, which handles the same core concept (does it really exist?) but goes for the paranoia angle instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the positive and constructive feedback, I moved the anomalous effect forward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do others agree that the SCP should be the missing town instead of the items in the box? I can make that happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted, because okay, it's a town that doesn't exist, I can dig that. But then nothing happens. Two encrypted text messages? Not frightening. In fact, they kill any creep for me because they are pretty much big neon signs for HEY LOOK A CONSPIRACY.

And, there's nothing particularly interesting about the location, except maybe that bit about the diner.

And finally, it really feels like the categorization is wrong. The scip here is not the three items. Those aren't anomalous. The anomaly is the fact that we have a town that does/doesn't exist. That should be the scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. It reminds me of paper towns. Mapmakers put fake towns into a map. If they see another map with the same fake town, they know that that map is plagiarized off of their map.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This idea is actually pretty interesting.
Plus it's not another tale or something like that, it feels like those are flooding in right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this, but the anomalous effect is introduced quite late (in the addendum). I would move that piece out of the addendum and put it into the description, personally. It is well-written and interesting though, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I have been schooled on commas this day. Should be fixed with the exception of the photo, which would not have one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to sound picky, but shouldn't Danhorn Iowa be Danhorn, Iowa?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really dig this style of sparseness for some reason. Keep working on it, though. I'm sure people who are more iffy on it will be able to point out things that I glossed over (such as Eskofart).

item.title: Looks like it went live before I was done
item.content:encoded: This is my first time with this editor. I assume I should have left it draft until I got all the bells and whistles working. It took me a while to figure out the photo upload procedure. I'll read SCP-1171 and try updating to address some of your comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually really like this, simply because it has no big twist. Just a few memories of a town that may or may not exist, like a song you can't quite remember. And no way to tell whether the town was erased from history, generated as a suggestion in people's minds, or something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your concept interests me, even if it is a little close to SCP-1173. But the execution is lacking; above and beyond grammar things (is Danhorn Iowa some strange alternate version of Iowa? Commatize thyself, foo'), the article is too short to pull off the conceit you're shooting for. You have to bury the lede a little bit.

What's interesting is that the places where you get a little more audacious — namely, the second item in containment — you don't fully explain the connection. The book has no connection to the town other than having been in the library, and yet nobody knows about it. If the library had the entire bibliography of Stephen King and the Bible, would there be no Langoliers or Christianity right now?

It's not a good article, but some polish could turn it into a very, very good article, if one similar to ones that have been done already.

EDIT: You know you don't have to include a picture div table if you don't have a picture, right?

DOUBLE EDIT: I MEANT SAMOTHRACE NOT BEAUREMONT DAMMIT
